citation.authors: Sean Kerman, Daniel Brown, Michael A. Goodrich
citation.title: Supporting Human Interaction with Robust Robot Swarms

study_type.justification: The paper's central, extensively simulation-validated contribution is a bio-inspired decentralized swarm control model with two attractors (torus, flock) and a 'stakeholder' mechanism letting a (conceptual) human operator switch between and lead these attractors, validated through parameter-sweep simulation studies (attractor-switching probability, path-following accuracy) with no human participant at any point, matching Algorithm/Controller Evaluation as Primary. A comparably-weighted secondary contribution is a formal linear stability analysis (change of variables, Jacobian linearization, eigenvalue analysis) proving local asymptotic stability of the cyclic (torus) equilibrium for an attraction-only simplified model, warranting Modeling/Theoretical Paper as Secondary.

summary.abstract_summary: This paper proposes a decentralized, bio-inspired swarm control model (extending Couzin et al.'s zone-based repulsion/orientation/attraction model, smoothed to avoid hard switching and adapted for nonholonomic robot dynamics) that exhibits two stable collective formations: a rotating 'torus' (useful for perimeter monitoring) and a translating 'flock' (useful for rapid relocation). Through simulation, the authors identify parameter values (notably the orientation-zone radius) at which both formations emerge with equal probability from random initial conditions, and through a formal linear stability analysis of a simplified attraction-only model, prove that the torus formation is a locally asymptotically stable limit cycle. The paper then introduces 'stakeholder' agents — swarm members simultaneously influenced by their neighbors and by a human-operator-broadcast reference input — and shows via simulation that a human needs only to influence a subset (roughly 30-40%) of the swarm to reliably switch it between torus and flock attractors, and can lead either formation along a moving reference path while preserving group structure, demonstrating a form of swarm control that is robust to limited/partial human-swarm communication.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: The swarm's task is to organize and control its own collective formation/motion (torus vs. flock, switching, leading); there is no external environmental entity, hazard, or target that the swarm detects, avoids, or acts upon.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: As no external task-relevant object exists in this model (see above), no behavioral relation to one applies; the described behaviors (forming, switching, and leading a torus/flock) are internal swarm self-organization, not a relation to an external object.

team_size.justification: The stakeholder mechanism is designed for a single human operator to broadcast a reference input to a subset of a 100-agent swarm; this is the model's conceptual use case, though not tested with an actual human participant.

team_composition.justification: All agents share identical dynamics and sensing/interaction rules; 'stakeholder' status is a control-influence distinction applied to a subset, not a difference in physical type or capability.
Only a single conceptual human operator is described; there is no multi-human team.

interaction_model.justification: The human operator's reference input is broadcast simultaneously to M stakeholder agents.

interaction_directness.justification: The reference input is explicitly broadcast to a defined set of M stakeholder agents.
Non-stakeholder swarm members are influenced only through their ordinary local interactions with stakeholders, not by anything addressed to them by the human.

communication_proximity.justification: The human operator's influence is modeled as a broadcast reference input, with no physical co-location described.

coordination_architecture.justification: All agents (including stakeholders) act on purely local repulsion/orientation/attraction interactions with visible neighbors; no leader robot or central coordinator exists.

computational_characteristics.justification: Agent steering is computed from zone-based repulsion/orientation/attraction force vectors toward neighbors, functionally equivalent to an artificial-potential-field method (the codebook's own source example for this code), rather than a population-based metaheuristic search or a trained model.

human_involvement_level.justification: The swarm self-organizes into a torus or flock formation autonomously via local rules with no human input required; a human may optionally monitor and intervene via stakeholders.

Successfully switching the swarm between attractors or leading it along a moving path requires the operator's continuous or triggered reference input to the stakeholders; without it, the swarm remains in its current attractor.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: Switching the swarm from one attractor to another is achieved by giving stakeholders a single discrete, fixed reference location; the swarm then autonomously transitions and stabilizes into the new formation.

Path-following requires the operator to continuously supply a moving reference position (a constant-velocity target) that stakeholders track in real time, with the swarm's own dynamics closing the inner formation-keeping loop.

operational_domain.justification: The model uses generic 2-D nonholonomic single-integrator agent dynamics without specifying a physical operational domain; the authors describe future work as 'implementation with real robot platforms' generically, without committing to air, land, or maritime.

flags.note: The abstract agent-dynamics model does not specify a physical operational domain (air/land/maritime); future work only vaguely mentions 'real robot platforms.'
